name: SE Grand County
short_description: Public BLM land in SE Grand County offers free rockhounding for agate, jasper, and petrified wood. Scenic desert terrain with excellent collecting opportunities and no permit required.
description:
SE Grand County sits on productive BLM public land where rockhounders can legally collect gemstones and minerals under the Federal Land Policy and Management Act. This region encompasses the red rock and high desert landscape characteristic of southeast Utah, with numerous washes, canyon bottoms, and hillside outcrops that expose agate nodules, colorful jasper fragments, and petrified wood specimens. The area is less crowded than nearby Moab destinations, making it ideal for serious collectors seeking solitude and quality finds. Desert varnish on many exposures helps highlight lighter-colored specimens, and seasonal water flow in washes continuously exposes fresh material. Access is straightforward with standard passenger vehicle routes to most productive zones, though some areas require short hiking or four-wheel-drive to reach optimal collecting spots.

The geology here reflects the Colorado Plateau's complex layering of Paleozoic through Cretaceous sedimentary formations. Colorful Jurassic mudstones and sandstones weather quickly, releasing agate and jasper nodules that accumulated over millions of years. Petrified wood occurs where ancient logs were buried and silicified by mineral-rich groundwater during the Mesozoic Era. The region's modern erosion from seasonal flash floods and long-term wind action continuously exposes new material on hillsides and wash banks.

Rockhounders should focus on canyon washes where water has concentrated material, and inspect the bases of colorful outcropping formations where specimens have weathered free. Early morning or late afternoon light makes it easier to spot translucent agates and the subtle color variations in jasper. The combination of free public access, reliable producing ground, and scenic high desert landscape makes SE Grand County a top-tier destination for Utah rockhounders of all skill levels.
history: This section of the Colorado Plateau formed during the Paleozoic and Mesozoic Eras when shallow inland seas deposited thick sequences of sandstones, siltstones, and mudstones. The silica-rich fluids that percolated through these formations during subsequent burial and uplift created the agate nodules and jasper replacements found throughout the region today. Modern erosion along the Colorado River drainage system and its tributaries has continuously exposed these mineralized zones for over a million years.
tips: Search washes and canyon bottoms after seasonal runoff - water concentration deposits specimens along banks and in gravel benches. Use a crack hammer to test colorful mudstone and sandstone outcrops for loose nodules before striking exposed rock. Look for translucent agate showing color layering under sunlight, and inspect the ground around weather-darkened exposure faces where specimens naturally separate. Carry a headlamp to examine specimens in shadow areas where color and translucency are easier to judge.
terrain_notes: Elevation ranges from 4,500 to 5,500 feet across rolling high desert terrain. Terrain is predominantly sandy and rocky with scattered juniper and pinyon pine. Walking distance from parking varies from immediate roadside collecting to 1-3 miles of hiking to remote outcrops. Footing is generally moderate with loose rock, sand, and some slickrock sections. Summer temperatures exceed 95 degrees F; winter highs reach 40-50 degrees F.
written_directions: From Moab, take US 191 south toward Monticello for approximately 25 miles. Turn east on County Road 115 (Dry Mesa Road) or similar BLM access routes marked on detailed topo maps. Continue on improved dirt road for 5-15 miles depending on target area. Multiple pullouts and informal parking areas exist along main washes and canyon approach routes. Consult current BLM Grand County maps for specific access points, as road conditions vary seasonally.
road_conditions: Improved dirt roads suitable for passenger vehicles in dry conditions. May become slippery or impassable after heavy rain. High-clearance vehicle recommended for remote access routes.
parking_notes: Multiple informal pullouts and parking areas along access roads near productive canyon washes. No developed parking facilities. Space available for 2-10 vehicles depending on location.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. Rock hammers and hand tools permitted. Specimens must fit within reasonable backpack limits. Leave no trace - fill holes, scatter tailings naturally.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 pounds per day
No commercial collection
No motorized equipment
Hand tools only
Leave no trace practices required
nearest_services: Moab, approximately 25 miles northwest. Full services including restaurants, lodging, equipment rental, and grocery stores.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat to gently rolling terrain with variable footing on sand and loose rock. Most productive areas accessible within short walks from parking. Some sites require hiking 1-3 miles on uneven terrain with no shade. Sturdy hiking boots recommended. Limited wheelchair access to immediate roadside collecting zones only.

faq.question: What types of agates and jasper can I find rockhounding in SE Grand County, Utah?
faq.answer: SE Grand County BLM land produces colorful red, orange, and cream-colored agates with distinct banding patterns, plus red and brown jasper fragments. Specimens typically range from 0.5 to 3 inches. Many agates show translucent qualities when held to light. Petrified wood with visible cellular structure is also common in exposed mudstone layers.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound on BLM land in Grand County, Utah?
faq.answer: No permit is required for personal rockhounding on BLM public land in SE Grand County. Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA) allows recreational collecting up to 25 pounds per day at no cost. Simply access public roads and marked BLM areas without advance permission.

faq.question: What is the best time of year for rockhounding near Moab in SE Grand County?
faq.answer: October through April offers the most comfortable rockhounding conditions. Summer temperatures exceed 95 degrees F with intense sun exposure. Winter is mild at these elevations, rarely dropping below freezing. Spring and fall provide ideal 60-75 degree temperatures with lower UV intensity.

faq.question: How far from Moab are the best rockhounding locations in SE Grand County?
faq.answer: Prime collecting areas start approximately 20-25 miles south of Moab via US 191 and BLM access roads. Most productive washes and outcrops are within 5-15 miles of main access routes. Day trips from Moab are practical for exploring multiple sites.

faq.question: Can families with children rockhound on BLM land in SE Grand County?
faq.answer: Yes, SE Grand County BLM land is excellent for families. Nearby roadside collecting requires minimal hiking, making it suitable for children ages 6 and up. Adults should supervise closely, watch for flash flood hazards in washes, and bring plenty of water. Hand tools only - no heavy equipment needed.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do I need for rockhounding in SE Grand County?
faq.answer: A rock hammer or small chisel works well for testing outcrops. Bring a sturdy backpack, water (at least 2-3 liters), sun protection, and hiking boots for uneven terrain. A hand lens helps identify agate translucency. Gloves protect against sharp flakes and cacti. GPS unit or detailed BLM map ensures safe navigation on remote roads.

meta_title: SE Grand County Rockhounding - Utah BLM
meta_description: Free rockhounding on BLM land near Moab. Find agate, jasper, and petrified wood. No permit required, 25 lbs per day limit. October - April best.
